entry.title: [Part 2] -- Prague Recap - Eurasmus & Parting
entry.summary:
Eurasmus?

From what I've heard about Eurasmus, is that the classes are significantly easier and the students' focus is about stretching your savings to cover as much traveling and drinking as possible.

I wouldn't say that the classes are "significantly easier"(maybe "less hard" is a better term). And yeah, traveling and social stuff (such as beer if one is in Prague) have a pretty high priority.

Eurasmus is about cultural exchange. People from all over the world came together here to learn something about other cultures. And having fun with each other turns out to be a great way to learn about other cultures.

Seems like a pretty good concept to me.

That's mostly okay with me, but at the same time I'm trying to take some of the core heavy-lifting courses for my major. I don't want to skimp out on the learning.

I'm so glad I got to take my core classes here. I don't think I missed enough academically to be concerned. Even if I did skimp a little on the academics, I think I'll be fine going into my senior year with everything I learned here.

Plus, I've been lucky enough to have a 9-week internship over the summer, and I usually learn more usefull stuff from a summer internship than a year of school anyways.

I'm moving forwards with a lot of energy and a balanced mindset.

^That's the spirit.

Parting

It's been tough saying bye to everyone I've met over the semester. It seems like every day for the past three weeks a friend has left to travel or go home. Pretty soon it will be only me and Tim staying for the internship over the summer. Part of the fun of this semester was that it couldn't last forever. So I came in already knowing that everything would end this summer, but I'm still bummed out a little bit.

We're moving accross town to an appartment in a few days, and we'll stay for the next eight weeks in Prague. I feel lucky to have the internship, but it will definitely be a change of pace. I think that come August, I'll be able to say goodbye to the city and go back home to get on to the next thing.

I hope that my path will cross again with some of the people that I've met in Prague.

entry.title: [Part 2] -- Prague Recap - Faculty
entry.summary:
The Faculty at CTU

I've only had a few interactions. The few prof's I met seemed to be relatively distant with no rush to be anywhere. The word aloof fits well.

I think that aloof fits European academia as a whole as opposed to the states, and not just for the professors. School feels a lot less frantic here. For example, during one of my finals, the professor let me know how many points I needed to pass, and encouraged me to stop once I answered enough questions so we could both go home early (I took his suggestion).

Maybe its because I just went to international classes, or maybe its because students really are just more chill here. Maybe it has something to do with the price of college being a fraction of it is in the states. If you fail a class here and need an extra semester, you just take it next year (and maybe pay a $500 tuition fee), but if you fail a class in the U.S. and need an extra semester, you're paying an extra $5k+ in tuition to retake it. The stakes are high.

Here, I have a seperate paper form keeping track of my student status, international student status, public transit, course record and rent payment. There's also two online accounts -- one for CUT and a seperate one for the Faculty of Electrical Engineering. If I want to change my thumbnail picture for either accounts, I can't just upload a picture. I have to visit another office. Those are really just trivial complaints so far. I'm still culture-shocked

^Now I disagree with that bolded bit. I don't think those complaints are trivial now. There's a good deal of B.S. bureaucracy to navigate and it takes more time and effort than I think should be spent on bureaucracy. but, hey, I survived it so how much can I really complain?

I appreciate that the school here keeps spending practical. I feel like Czech culture is a lot more focused on practicality. Why take your car to the garage when you can learn how to fix it yourself?

I haven't seen any stupid $90M buildings here that don't have any classrooms. I found one! On the east side of campus there is a 9-story glass office building for the school.

I haven't seen the labs yet. I don't think these will differ much. I mostly liked the computer labs here. Some ran Linux, others ran Windows. They usually had a common password to log in. One lab needed a hardware update pretty badly (Windows 7 sticker on the case and trying to run Windows 10). Another lab had some very nice Zynq embedded development boards for the whole class.

I also haven't seen any of the classrooms I'll have. It was a little surprising to me that there wasn't some kind of tour around the Electrical Engineering buildings, but thats ok. I'll be happier figuring it out on my own anyways.

^Preach. I had a ton of fun trying to figure out where to go. On my first day of the semester, for my first class, I went to the room I thought I was supposed to go to. And nobody was there. I hung around for 15 minutes than tried to ask a cook at the cafeteria if I was at the right place. Turns out my class was on a different campus 20 minutes away by metro, but the surprise was all part of the fun.

entry.title: [Part 2] -- Prague Recap - Students Here
entry.summary:
The Students at CTU

I can't say enough good things about the Czech students at CTU that I've met so far.

^ I wrote that above back in February, and I'd extend it to all of the Czech students that I met throughout the year. I met an incredible amount of international students too. I'm proud to call so many interesting and dynamic people my friend this semester, and I sincerely hope we cross paths again one day.

There was something interesting to do every day with all sorts of interesting people. I don't think it would do anyone justice to try to write about every one of them, because I don't want to write that much and I'd definitely leave something out. I guess the common factor was that we all had strong individual personalities and a lot of energy, but got along well.

Again, the people here were what made studying abroad great for me.

They tell me that a huge amount of young people in Prague speak English, but fewer of the older and rural people speak English.

I found out that a huge amount of people in Europe speak English. One time I was waiting for a bathroom at a train-station in rural Czech Republic, and a scruffy guy in overalls says something to me in Czech. I was spacing out, and asked him to say it again (in Czech), but then he stopped a moment and started again in English.

Anyways, Czech is a hard language to learn.

I still agree. Czech is a hard language for a native english speaker to learn. I've been more interested in learning in the past month since my classes have started to chill out. Czech grammar is pretty foreign, and I think its interesting. I found a few students that study full-time here, but don't know much Czech, so I think that its sort of 'optional' to learn.

Most of the bars/clubs have menus in English anyways, and respond to me in English when I try to open with "pivo prosim."

This is still true, even in Czech cities other than Prague. I really appreciate that its easy for English speakers to get around in the Czech Republic, but its also really satisfying to communicate something with someone in their native language. It's not too often you get to learn a foreign language in the country that its spoken in, so I'm going to keep at it until I go home.

Most people that I talk to seem more interested in American music, movies and politics than I am.

This trend continued throughout the year. Last week this guy from Italy started chatting me up about the Celtics, and man, he knew everything that happened since Larry Bird. Puts me to shame.

Yes, I did hear a lot of Trump comments. The Trump comments peaked during March and gradually tapered off throughout the year. I'm greatful, because I would rather talk about the mold on the ceiling than the latest tweet from the commander in chief.

entry.title: [Part 2] -- Prague Recap
entry.summary:
At the beginning, I planned on only writing two blog entries, but I don't think I can keep this follow-up post short enough to not be obnoxious. I've written a page for each section in the original article that covers that part of the experience.

Click through the links to read more.

What's Happened So Far in One Semester?

Descriptions of my favorite "haiku moments" from Europe.

If I tried to write down how every weekend went I'd probably call it quits instead of finishing a blog post. So instaed, I took my some of my favorite moments of the semester and wrote them out as shortly as possible.

The Students at CTU

Just a little bit about some of the friends I made in Prague.

Man, I really liked the people that I met in Prague. I think that the people here were my #1 favorite thing about this semester. I don't want to embarass anyone by talking about them directly, or by mentinoing specifics, but I still want to leave an impression of how the people here were so much fun.

The Faculty at CTU

Focusing on how classes went at CTU

Classes turned out to be more of a secondary (or maybe tertiary) focus of the semester, but that doesn't mean that they weren't still an important part! I took the classes relatively seriously and got something useful out of them, but they definitely differed from classes at UML.

Predictions/How did that "strategy" turn out?

I answer the predictions I made

Some predictions were spot on, while others were not :)

Eurasmus/Parting

Some final words on studying abroad and why you should too.

TLDR; Prague was great. I learned a lot, and made some great friends. I'm ready to go home now, but I'll keep this with me for the rest of my life.

entry.title: [Part 2] -- Prague Recap - Haiku moments
entry.summary:
A Haiku moment

Haiku moment:

"an aesthestic moment' of a timeless feeling ~~of enlightened harmony as the poet's nature and the environment are unified~~" - Kenneth Yasuda (corny parts struck through)

So here are some of my haiku moments.

Pidgeons in Brussels

On a trip, I stayed in Brussels for two days and decided all my calories would come from chocolate, waffles and fries.

At the end I found myself with 2 extra hours before having to leave for the airport. I ordered one last waffle (my fifth of the day) and watched the sunset at the Jardin du Monts des Arts.

I noticed two pidgeons fighting over a waffle. This wasn't one of the chalky 1-Euro chalky waffles that, but one of the good 3-Euro gaufres. The waffle was elastic enough to keep the pidgeons from tearing it apart. But the two pidgeons kept at it until they struggled out of sight with the waffle.

I like to think that they're still trying to eat it today.

International Squad Goodbye Party

I got invited to a friend of a friend's goodbye party. My group was the first to show up, and we got chatting with the hosts. By the time I left there must have been 45 people in his appartment, and everyone was speaking English. I was the only native English speaker there.

On my way out I realized that when I left they weren't speaking English just for me, and I'm not sure why I would have thought otherwise.

Drunk patronizing the vending machine until it breakes

Spare change and alcohol were my worst enemy. In the hall between the dorm's lobby and my bed, there is a vending machine that sells chocolate bars. Normally, I just look at it.

But, coming home after a few beers, I always check my pockets for spare change to get a candy bar.

By May I had tried every European candy bar in the machine, but also in May, the machine stopped working.

It wouldn't take any coins, even if you feed them in three or four times. Probably to my benefit.

Just being content sitting still

Went to the Letna park to picnic with friends a few times. Was really just content sitting on the grass and talking about nothing. These were some of the best days.

Practicing Czech by ordering beer

At the Letna beer gardens, they sell dark Kozel (my favorite commercial Czech beer). Every time I've been there, I've tried to complete a transaction entirely in Czech and Czech is hard.

Round 1

me

"Jedo pivo prosim"

bartender

"Which kind?"

fuck. English == I made a mistake.

me

"I'll have a half liter of dark Kozel please" bartender "thirty nine crowns please"

Round 2

Ok, have to specify what kind of beer.

me

"Dobry den, dam si jedno cerny Kozel prosim."

bartender

"... The big or the small one"

fuck

me

"I'll have the big one"

bartender

"thirty nine crowns"

Round 3

Maybe the next time, I'll cut out everything but the essentials?

me

"Jedno ... velky cerny Kozel prosim"

bartender

"thirty nine crowns"

fuck

Round 4

This time, I'll use the right gender adjective.

me

"Jeden velky cerny Kozel prosim"

bartender

"... thirty nine crowns"

fuck

I'm pretty sure I got that last one gramatically correct. Maybe it's just that my pronunciation was too far off. I'll keep trying throughout the summer though and maybe I'll update if I get it right.

Update July 9th

I finally got it. The secret is to not order dark beer .

(or maybe my pronunciation just got better)

me

"Jeden velky Gambrinus prosim"

bartender

"Tricet korun"

Success

German trains aren't on atomic time

On my way to Belgium I took a detour through Germany that involved maybe five train transfers. Two of the transfers involved 5 minute layovers. "Not a problem," I thought. German trains are never late.

One of the trains was 3 minutes late to one of those critical transfers. I spent 30 seconds at the station double checking which umlauted train name was the one I wanted,

then the next 30 seconds second guessing myself,

then the last 30 seconds speed walking towards the train I needed to get on as it left the station.

This actually didn't turn out to be a big deal because the info desk hooked me up with a bus to the next station I needed.

The second hairwidth transfer was also 3 minutes late. No indicision or second guessing this time; I learned that lesson.

I speedwalked all the way accross the platform. (Next time I'll briskly jog).

I got caught between the doors of my next train as they were closing. When stuck on something soft between them, the doors of German ICE trains close with a surprising ammount of force before opening again to let the soft object pull himself and his backpack onto the train.

Heroes of the Hydrich terror vs. Technogym

Technogym is an Italian fitness company that made some really un-ergonomic isolation training gear in the previous millennium. CUT's K.N. campus has an okay weightroom in the basement that could be good if they got rid of all the 80's Technogym junk that nobody uses anyways. I had an hour time slot ever week where I could go work out before Wenesday classes.

During Nazi occupation of Prague, Czech underground soldiers assasinate Reinhard Heydrich, #3 ranking Nazi, in Prague. The assasins were betrayed and eventually were forced to hole up in the crypt of a church as 800 Nazi soliders tried to force them out. The standoff ended after the Nazis started flooding the crypt with water and the assasins committed suicide.

On the map, these two basements are no more than 90m apart from eachother.

entry.title: [Part 2] -- Prague Recap - Predictions
entry.summary:
Here's me responding to the speculations I made at the beginning of the semester

Will lecture attendance be mandatory?

Not a chance.

Spot on.

My Economics professor was very straightforward about lectures being completely optional. On more than one occasion, I've been the only one who goes to a lecture. On more than one occasion, I've skipped a lecture. One time, one of my friends was 45 minutes late to a 2 hour lecture for no good reason. In retrospect, I probably could have skipped one or two of the less useful lectures more often.

Will the textbooks be cheaper?

Yes, but I'm also guessing that piracy will be more common.

I actually missed my window to buy the English version of recommended textbooks from the book store, so I can't give a good answer.

That being said, none of the instructors required a textbook and only one or two listed one as recommended. I didn't really hear anyone talking about pirating textbooks this semester. I can't tell if that's because it wasn't a big enough deal to talk about, or nobody studying abroad needs textbooks in the first place

To be fair, I only talked to foreign students about this, and only a few times.

One German guy told me that his most expensive textbook was something close to $55, which I thought was funny.

Will final exams revolve around rote memorization or concepts?

Tough one, but I think that finals will be mostly conceptual.

There was a good mix of both.

The final for Microcontrollers was 11 or 12 questions (one for each lecture) asking for basically a summary of that lecture -- kind of bland rote learning imho, but at least the lectures involved some cool hands on examples with one of those cool PIC development boards.

At the final for Applied Electronics, our professor brought us down to the computer lab and had us design a PCB from drawing the schematic, to drawing component footprints to routing traces through the PCB. This was anything but rote.

My Computer Architecture class will have a verbal component to the final.

The verbal component turned out to be a second chance on questions you goofed up. After submitting my exam, the professor called me over to his desk to ask a few questions that started with "Are you sure that you meant to..."

I appreciated this part of the exam, because I completely misinterpreted one of the questions and was able to prove that I knew it verbally. I don't think this system would scale well to classes larger than 8 people.

I'd be pretty pissed if that means I have to memorize the MIPS instruction set to regurgitate on demand.

I wasn't required to memorize it, but I benifited a lot from remembering a good deal of it. It seemed like a reasonable ammount of detail to expect.

Will there be a greater stress on student independence than in the U.S.?

Absolutely.

Nailed it.

I felt so much more independent than in class at UML.

The mentality here is that it's pretty much on your responsibility to learn what you have to. Professors are helpful (mostly) when you need to ask a question, but there was very little hand-holding going on during the parts where I actually learned. This goes along with the fact that lectures were optional.

Will classes overall be easier or harder than UMass Lowell?

I think the mean class here will be more difficult than UML.

Nahhh.

I think that the philosophy of Erasmus classes puts formal education temporarily behind multicultural exchange. Don't get me wrong, I learned a sh*t ton from Computer Architectures and Operating Systems (my two toughest classes), but the workload was less demanding than at UML.

Mostly because the courses I am taking are just more difficult subjects, and partially because some of the Erasmus students hadn't really heard the term "Easy-A." I don't think that joke classes are a "thing" here.

The European term for an "Easy-A" is an "Erasmus course." Maybe not an Easy-A, but an Easy-Pass, because most professors will make it very clear how much work you need to do pass and will remind you how many points you need to get on the final for a 'C' if you ask.

So maybe an "Easy-A" isn't the same as "Erasmus-easy." Instead of participation credit, I would have just one moderately difficult exam that counted for the majority of my grade and multiple chances to retake it.

Here's what happened to the priorities I outlined at the beginning

I've got a list of priorities for the way I spend my time that I plan to stick to:

  1. Travel
  2. Sleep
  3. Academics
  4. Social
  5. Exercise
  6. Down time

The semester ended with the following:

  1. Social
  2. Travel
  3. Sleep
  4. Academics
  5. Exercise
  6. Down time

Basically social interactions jumped from the 4-spot on the list to the top. I felt like the most interesting parts about this semester came from some of the people I met, from a meme-lord from Kansas to a graduate student from Syria.

I think I traveled somewhere five out of the first six weekends, but after just a few weekends in Prague hanging out with the other exchange students, I started to value that interaction a whole lot more.

Excercise-wise, I did enough to feel good. I ran regularly until tweaking my knee during the Prague Half Marathon, and haven't really gone on a long run since. I might have put on 10 pounds, but I don't regret it.

I want to travel every single weekend and milk the life out of the nights. This might be my only time ever in Europe, and I don't want to feel like I missed out on anything.

That was fun for the first month or so, but that kind of travel schedule takes a toll on you for a while. After a while, I didn't want to travel every weekend, but I definitely kept milking the life out of this semester. Like I said, some of the best days I had were just sitting in the parks of Prague with friends and doing nothing.

And I will come back to Europe for sure. Hopefully I'll see some of the friends I met another day.

I don't feel like I missed out on anything.

My pseudo-imperative rules pretty much got shredded along the way.

Asside from that I'll keep the same general personal rules that I do at UML.

  • Never skip class unless something MUCH better comes up.

I slept in once or twice through an early class and maybe dropped down to 50% attendance on a few lectures I really didn't like. This part turned out to be a pretty minor deal, because attendance was anything but mandatory and I could study more effectively on my own.

  • Do homework as soon as possible and study some every weekday.

I wound up studying a little every day, but I also found myself pushing projects off until a little too late for comfort. There were better things to do, and in the end I passed my classes.

  • 8 hours of sleep should be normal every night.

Sleep was a big deal for me before coming over. I think I'm averaging about 6:30h / night now at the end of the semester. There's a lot of factors working against good sleep: international travel is much cheaper at odd times of day, night life doesn't really get going until 11:30pm, and my brain seemed pretty much hardwired to wake up at 8:00am at the latest.

Anyways, I felt like this semester lived up to my expectations while still surprising me.

entry.title: [Part 1] -- Expectations of Electrical Engineering Classes in Prague
entry.summary:
I'm an American exchange student studying Electrical Engineering at Czech Technical Institute in Prague with 200+ other international students for the Spring semester of 2017. This article covers my first impressions and expectations one week after arrival. Part 2 will compare my expectations with my experience and then identify the differences between EE courses in the USA.

I'm limiting this series to only two posts so I'll stay motivated to actually follow up

What's Happened So Far in One Week?

Not a whole lot (academically speaking).

I arrived, moved in to my dorm, registered for courses and toured the buildings. Most of the assistance I've received has been from the International Student Club (ISC) as opposed to staff, which impressed me.

The faculty here seems to be dependent on paper forms (which did not impress me). I've had to physically cut and paste a picture of myself onto 5 different documents. Moving into the dorm involved paying first month's rent in cash -- $175 (less than a third than a modest Lowell appartment). Financially, life is good for a U.S. exchange student.

What I can't really judge yet is the classes themselves, but I'll go out on a limb and extrapolate a bit.

The Students at CTU

I can't say enough good things about the Czech students at CTU that I've met so far. Every interaction has been positive. A student picked me up from the airport and another brought me to the metro office and helped me buy a public-transportation pass; another helped me get my student ID. Then socially, I met a handful of other Czech students at the pub that were really pretty friendly.

They tell me that a huge ammount of young people in Prague speak English, but fewer of the older and rural people speak English. I guess this is the case with quite a bit of Europe. Of the 200+ exchange students, only about 25 are American. The rest are from all over. Most people that I talk to seem more interested in American music, movies and politics than I am. Which is cool.

Anyways, Czech is a hard language to learn. Most of the international students don't seem interested in learning Czech. Most of the bars/clubs have menus in English anyways, and respond to me in English when I try to open with "pivo prosim."

The student clubs at CTU have been noticably different from the clubs at home. For example, there is a student club that maintains the facilities in my dormotory. I can join for $20 and get access to the wired internet, gym room and printing service that they maintain.

The ISC is something else too. They've put together a handful of "school-sponsored parties" already. Whenever I hear school-sponsored, I usually recoil a little bit, because that usually means sub-par catering, a powerpoint presentation and an attendance of 12 +/- 5. But no, "school sponsored" here means that the ISC reserves an entire bar or club and then hundreds of international students show up to drink beer together. Its quite a bit more in line with what students actually want to do.

Part of the difficulty of the U.S. "School sponsored party" is the drinking age in the states. That's something that the Europeans I've met like to critique. I only turned 21 the week of my flight to the Czech Republic. I've never been to an American bar. But man, the Prague bars are a really good time. Beer is cheaper than bottled water too, so, financially, its pretty reasonable to go have a beer after class every day (by the way, my dorm has a bar in the basement and a pub on the same block).

The Czech Republic has an enormous beer culture. You would be very hard pressed to go without drinking a beer anywhere you go. Every social interaction seems to revolve around beer (I went to a rock climbing gym that had a bar built-in). There isn't a heavy binge drinking culture though that I've noticed. Usually the only people I've seen order hard liquor at the bar have been the international students.

Back to academics. I had to digress about beer because it is such an integral part of the culture here. There's a lot of evidence of some very motivated students. I've seen 4 different beautiful SAE student-made race cars on the CTU campus, one might have been electric too. I'm talking really beautiful cars. Cars with three spoilers with flawless molded fiberglass bodies with stripes over glossy black paint (instead of exposed painted JV-welded frames that seem to be more common). I even saw what looked like a piece of a student-built single-person jet in a hallway. All along the hallways were display cases filled with examples of student projects that were actually beautiful. Not just functional but aesthetic. When I get back, I'll be a little more motivated to step up my capstone project to bring some of that artistic talent back to the Ball 4th floor hallway. I would really like to meet someone from their design teams to try to figure out what makes their culture so successful.

The Faculty at CTU

I've only had a few interactions. The few prof's I met seemed to be relatively distant with no rush to be anywhere. The word aloof fits well.

We heard an introductory speech from a handful of the professors so far. They talked slowly, but with very good English (If I had to judge, I'd guess that the median professor here has better english than the median professor at home). They did some very "professorly" things -- like post important URLs on powerpoints that were not electronically available to us (the kind of thing you see and then try to scribble down before they advance the slide).

Things happen less efficiently here than in the states. It took 20 minutes at a window to get my public transportation pass. Our welcome day speech from the professors included two 30-minute coffee breaks (the speaches themselves probably took 3-4 hours). At home, I like having one single student card attached to one central student account that I can use to pay for lunch, access my class buildings, get into the gym etc. Here, I have a seperate paper form keeping track of my student status, international student status, public transit, course record and rent payment. There's also two online accounts -- one for CUT and a seperate one for the Faculty of Electrical Engineering. If I want to change my thumbnail picture for either accounts, I can't just upload a picture. I have to visit another office.

Those are really just trivial complaints so far. I'm still culture-shocked I appreciate that the school here keeps spending practical. I haven't seen any stupid $90M buildings here that don't have any classrooms.

I haven't seen the labs yet. I signed a generic lab-safety form that looked exactly like the ones in the US. I don't think these will differ much.

I also haven't seen any of the classrooms I'll have. It was a little surprising to me that there wasn't some kind of tour around the Electrical Engineering buildings, but thats ok. I'll be happier figuring it out on my own anyways.

Learning Philosophy

Erasmus?

Erasmus is a word I learned from the European students. It is a program where your government pays you to study abroad in another European country. The majority of the students here are "on Erasmus."

From what I've heard about Erasmus, is that the classes are significantly easier and the students focus is about stretching your savings to cover as much traveling and drinking as possible.

That's mostly okay with me, but at the same time I'm trying to take some of the core heavy-lifting courses for my major. I don't want to skimp out on the learning.

I'm moving forwards with a lot of energy and a balanced mindset.

Predictions

I'm predicting the following things about the upcoming semester:

Will lecture attendance be mandatory?

Not a chance.

Will the textbooks be cheaper?

Yes, but I'm also guessing that piracy will be more common.

Will final exams revolve around rote memorization or concepts?

Tough one, but I think that finals will be mostly conceptual. My Computer Architecture class will have a verbal component to the final. I'd be pretty pissed if that means I have to memorize the MIPS instruction set to regurgitate on demand.

Will there be a greater stress on student independence than in the U.S.?

Absolutely.

Will classes overall be easier or harder than UMass Lowell?

I think the mean class here will be more difficult than UML. Mostly because the courses I am taking are just more difficult subjects, and partially because some of the Erasmus students hadn't really heard the >term "Easy-A." I don't think that joke classes are a "thing" here.

The Strategy

I've got a list of priorities for the way I spend my time that I plan to stick to:

  • Travel
  • Sleep
  • Academics
  • Social
  • Exercise
  • Down time

I want to travel every single weekend and milk the life out of the nights. This might be my only time ever in Europe, and I don't want to feel like I missed out on anything.

Asside from that I'll keep the same general personal rules that I do at UML. - Never skip class unless something MUCH better comes up. - Do homework as soon as possible and study some every weekday. - 8 hours of sleep should be normal every night.

Parting

As I write this I'm trying to log into one of the online accounts to take a look at my schedule for the first time. Hoping for no conflicts.

This week has been a lot of real good social stuff. I already have travels lined up for the next few weekends, and I'm ready to take on some classes. Everything is looking good.

Check out the Follow-up Post I made in July
